JS From Zero EP.8 — Anonymous Functions and IIFEs
Day 8 of getting reincarnated into JavaScript. Last time we covered functions, so this time we're looking at anonymous functions.
An anonymous function is basically just a
. In the last post we saw how a named function is defined, like this ↓

So an anonymous function is pretty easy to wrap your head around — if there's no function name, that's what we call an anonymous function. The most common way you'll see one is stored in a variable. When you do that, the function doesn't get hoisted (I'll cover hoisting in a later chapter). Here's the usual way anonymous functions are defined ↓
And sure, if you really want to, you can write it this way and still give it a name:
But honestly there's no point — you're just making yourself type more for nothing.
There's another kind of anonymous function called an IIFE, short for
. It's also a flavor of anonymous function, and you usually define it like this ↓

In short, an IIFE runs the moment the code executes — you don't have to call it yourself to trigger it. The invocation parentheses at the end don't have a fixed spot. One way is to put them like this ↓
The other way is to put them inside the parentheses ↓
Both work — it comes down to whatever you're used to. But if you want to pass a value into the IIFE, you'd do it like this ↓
There's another nice perk to using an IIFE: it doesn't pollute the global scope. Everything defined inside it stays unique to that scope. Here's a classic example. When we run a loop 10 times,
But what if we don't want
to end up as 10? What if we want 0...9 instead? We can use an IIFE for that:
And if you go digging through frameworks, you'll find a lot of them use the IIFE trick too — jQuery and Vue, for example.

Day 8 of getting reincarnated into JavaScript. Last time we covered functions, so this time we're looking at anonymous functions — functions with no name. We'll also dig into IIFEs (immediately invoked function expressions), why they don't pollute the global scope, and the classic loop-with-setTimeout gotcha.
